brief_document.title: Packaging, publishing metadata, READMEs, and seven-operation examples for all four SDKs
brief_document.purpose: Make all four SDKs adoptable with a one-line dependency add. Each gets complete registry metadata, a README that teaches the seven operations, and a runnable example that exercises every operation against a server. Without this, the SDKs exist but no one can find, install, or learn them.
brief_document.task: Finalise packaging and documentation for aion-client (crates.io), aion-client-python (PyPI), aion-client-typescript (npm), and aion_client (Hex). Each package: complete metadata (name, version, description, license, repository, keywords/categories, authors), a README walking through connect + the seven operations, and a runnable example program covering start/signal/query/cancel/list/describe/subscribe against a server. This brief does not change SDK behaviour; it packages and documents what AL-002..AL-006 built.

requirements.title: Registry metadata for all four packages
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL complete the publish metadata for each package so it is uploadable to its registry. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT leave a placeholder version, description, license, or repository field.
acceptance: crates/aion-client/Cargo.toml has name aion-client, a version, description, license, repository, keywords, and categories.
sdks/python/aion-client/pyproject.toml has distribution name aion-client-python, version, description, license, project URLs, and classifiers; the import package is aion_client.
sdks/typescript/aion-client/package.json has name aion-client-typescript, version, description, license, repository, main/module/types entry points, and a files allowlist.
gleam/aion_client/gleam.toml has name aion_client, version, description, licences, and repository links.
No metadata field is a TODO/placeholder; a dry-run publish/pack for each (cargo package, python -m build, npm pack, gleam build) succeeds.

requirements.title: READMEs teaching the seven operations
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a README per package that shows connect plus each of the seven operations with a typed payload. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT ship a README that omits any of the seven operations or the error-handling pattern.
acceptance: Each README has an install line, a connect snippet, and a snippet per operation (start, signal, query, cancel, list, describe, subscribe) in that language.
Each README shows the typed payload path and mentions the raw escape hatch.
Each README shows catching/branching on at least two error-taxonomy variants idiomatically.
Each README links to the runnable example from R3 and states the server-URL prerequisite.
The Rust README is the crate-level doc (//! or README referenced from Cargo.toml) so it renders on docs.rs and crates.io.

requirements.title: Runnable seven-operation examples
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL ship a runnable example per package that connects to a server and exercises all seven operations end to end. THE SYSTEM SHALL surface errors from the taxonomy rather than crashing opaquely.
acceptance: crates/aion-client/examples/ has an example binary that connects, starts a workflow, signals it, queries it, lists, describes, cancels, and subscribes to its events, reading the server URL from an env var / arg.
sdks/python/aion-client/examples/ and sdks/typescript/aion-client/examples/ have the equivalent runnable scripts.
gleam/aion_client/test/example_test.gleam (or an examples module) provides the equivalent runnable Gleam flow.
Each example handles at least one error-taxonomy variant explicitly (e.g. prints a clear message on Unavailable) rather than panicking/throwing uncaught.
Each example is referenced from its README and runs against the AL-007 server fixture.

boundaries: SHALL NOT change SDK behaviour or public APIs — packaging and docs only.
SHALL NOT publish to the live registries as part of the brief — it makes the packages publishable (dry-run verified).
SHALL NOT introduce a fifth-language package.
verification: cargo package -p aion-client succeeds; the crate README renders.
python -m build succeeds for clients/python; the long_description resolves to the README.
npm pack succeeds for clients/typescript and includes the README and built artefacts per the files allowlist.
gleam build succeeds for clients/gleam and the package docs build.
Each README demonstrates all seven operations and at least one error-handling branch; each example runs against the conformance server fixture.

checklist.text: aion-client (crates.io), aion-client-python (PyPI), aion-client-typescript (npm), and aion_client (Hex) each carry complete package metadata, a README, and a type-checked public surface.
Each package ships a runnable example exercising all seven operations against a server, referenced from its README.

stories.text: As a platform team, I want each client published to its native registry with a README and a runnable example so that adopting it is a one-line dependency add.

constraints.text: Clients consume the aion-server API and aion-proto wire types; they SHALL NOT define their own wire formats, endpoints, or fields the server does not serve. The protocol is owned by AW.

Clients SHALL NOT depend on engine internals (aion, aion-store, beamr). The Rust client may depend on aion-core (shared domain types) and aion-proto; the embedded constructor may depend on aion only behind a feature flag, never by default.
All four SDKs expose the same seven operations with identical observable behaviour, verified by the shared conformance suite.
Every SDK exposes both a typed payload surface (per language) and a raw-Payload escape hatch; neither is removable.
Every SDK maps server failures to a documented, branchable error taxonomy. No swallowed errors, no single catch-all collapsing distinct failures.

subscribe resumes transparently across transient disconnects using the per-event sequence number, delivering a gap-free duplicate-free stream; terminal failures surface through the stream, not as a silent end.
start honours a caller-supplied idempotency key: a retried identical start returns the original handle; a conflicting reuse raises AlreadyExists.

Per-ecosystem toolchain gates pass: Rust (clippy -D warnings, fmt --check, no allow/expect/ignore), Python (ruff + mypy --strict + pytest), TypeScript (tsc strict + eslint + tests), Gleam (gleam format --check + gleam check + gleam test). No-partial-implementation and no-silent-failure standards hold across all four.
Each package is publishable to its registry (crates.io, PyPI, npm, Hex) with a README, a type-checked public surface, and a runnable example covering all seven operations.

resolved_context.intention: Ship the caller-side SDKs for the workflow-caller role: aion-client (Rust, crates.io), aion-client-python (PyPI), aion-client-typescript (npm), and aion_client (Gleam, Hex). Each connects to an aion-server deployment and exposes the same seven operations — connect, start, signal, query, cancel, list, describe, subscribe — in the idioms of its language. A developer who has never read the engine internals should add a dependency, point at a server URL, and start a workflow with full type-checking in their language within minutes; a frontend developer using the TypeScript client should never see beamr, Gleam, or Rust. The four SDKs must behave identically where behaviour is observable, proven by a shared language-neutral behavioural contract and a conformance suite that exercises every SDK against a real server.
